sections.paragraph:
ἀνανήφω 1 aor. ἀνένηψα (Aristot. et al.) ‘become sober’ (rather oft. transferred to the spiritual, esp. ethical realm in later Gk.: Cebes 9, 3; Dio Chrys. 4, 77; Ps.-Lucian, Salt. 84; M. Ant. 6, 31; Philo, Leg. All. 2, 60 ἀνανήφει, τοῦτο δʼ ἐστὶ μετανοεῖ; Jos., Ant. 6, 241; s. Nägeli 30; s. νήφω) come to one’s senses again ἀ. ἐκ τῆς τοῦ διαβόλου παγίδος come to one’s s. and escape from the snare of the devil 2 Ti 2:26. Abs. ἀνανῆψαι become sober again ISm.

aor. aor. = aorist

Aristot Aristot , various works, IV b.c.—List 5

et al. et al. = et alii (and others)

oft. oft. = often

esp. esp. = especially

Gk. Gk. = Greek

Cebes Cebes , prominent in Platonic dialogues; ref. is to a dialogue associated with his name, I a.d.—List 5

Dio Chrys Dio Chrys , I–II a.d.—List 5

Ps.-Lucian Ps.-Lucian , after II a.d.—List 5

  1. M. Ant. = Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, II a.d.—List 5

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

Jos. Jos. = Josephus. This abbr. used when follow by title; I a.d.—Lists 5

ISm ISm = Ignatius to the Smyrnaeans, I–II a.d.—List 1

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 68
